title: Handicap allowances: why you don't always get 100%
microFact: Formats trim your handicap: ~95% in singles Stableford, 85% in four-ball. It protects fields from hot high-handicappers.
lesson:
Competition small print that surprises new players: you rarely play off your FULL Course Handicap. Each format applies an ALLOWANCE (recommended by WHS):

• Singles stroke play / Stableford: 95%
• FOUR-BALL better-ball: 85% each
• Foursomes: 50% of the pair's COMBINED
• Scrambles: various team formulas (e.g., 25/20/15/10% of the four players)

Why: statistics. In big fields, higher handicaps have more score VARIANCE — more chances someone shoots the round of their life. Allowances level the prize table so low handicappers aren't structurally locked out.

So when the Bangalore corporate event sheet says "Format: Individual Stableford, 95% handicap allowance," you now know: Course Handicap 31 → playing handicap 29 (0.95 × 31, rounded). The starter's sheet or app does the math; your job is only to not be confused when your number differs from your Index — and maybe to explain it quietly to the next confused newcomer, which is exactly how club social capital gets built.

quiz.question: Why do four-ball events give only ~85% of course handicap?
options: High handicappers' score variance gives their team outsized best-ball chances
Because two players share the strokes
quiz.explanation: A high handicapper only needs a few career holes to lift a best-ball score — the allowance rebalances the odds.
